{"content": "     1|---
     2|sidebar_position: 6
     3|title: "Event Hooks"
     4|description: "Run custom code at key lifecycle points — log activity, send alerts, post to webhooks"
     5|---
     6|
     7|# Event Hooks
     8|
     9|Hermes has three hook systems that run custom code at key lifecycle points:
    10|
    11|| System | Registered via | Runs in | Use case |
    12||--------|---------------|---------|----------|
    13|| **[Gateway hooks](#gateway-event-hooks)** | `HOOK.yaml` + `handler.py` in `~/.hermes/hooks/` | Gateway only | Logging, alerts, webhooks |
    14|| **[Plugin hooks](#plugin-hooks)** | `ctx.register_hook()` in a [plugin](/docs/user-guide/features/plugins) | CLI + Gateway | Tool interception, metrics, guardrails |
    15|| **[Shell hooks](#shell-hooks)** | `hooks:` block in `~/.hermes/config.yaml` pointing at shell scripts | CLI + Gateway | Drop-in scripts for blocking, auto-formatting, context injection |
    16|
    17|All three systems are non-blocking — errors in any hook are caught and logged, never crashing the agent.
    18|
    19|## Gateway Event Hooks
    20|
    21|Gateway hooks fire automatically during gateway operation (Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, Teams) without blocking the main agent pipeline.
    22|
    23|### Creating a Hook
    24|
    25|Each hook is a directory under `~/.hermes/hooks/` containing two files:
    26|
    27|```text
    28|~/.hermes/hooks/
    29|└── my-hook/
    30|    ├── HOOK.yaml      # Declares which events to listen for
    31|    └── handler.py     # Python handler function
    32|```
    33|
    34|#### HOOK.yaml
    35|
    36|```yaml
    37|name: my-hook
    38|description: Log all agent activity to a file
    39|events:
    40|  - agent:start
    41|  - agent:end
    42|  - agent:step
    43|```
    44|
    45|The `events` list determines which events trigger your handler. You can subscribe to any combination of events, including wildcards like `command:*`.
    46|
    47|#### handler.py
    48|
    49|```python
    50|import json
    51|from datetime import datetime
    52|from pathlib import Path
    53|
    54|LOG_FILE = Path.home() / ".hermes" / "hooks" / "my-hook" / "activity.log"
    55|
    56|async def handle(event_type: str, context: dict):
    57|    """Called for each subscribed event. Must be named 'handle'."""
    58|    entry = {
    59|        "timestamp": datetime.now().isoformat(),
    60|        "event": event_type,
    61|        **context,
    62|    }
    63|    with open(LOG_FILE, "a") as f:
    64|        f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")
    65|```
    66|
    67|**Handler rules:**
    68|- Must be named `handle`
    69|- Receives `event_type` (string) and `context` (dict)
    70|- Can be `async def` or regular `def` — both work
    71|- Errors are caught and logged, never crashing the agent
    72|
    73|### Available Events
    74|
    75|| Event | When it fires | Context keys |
    76||-------|---------------|--------------|
    77|| `gateway:startup` | Gateway process starts | `platforms` (list of active platform names) |
    78|| `session:start` | New messaging session created | `platform`, `user_id`, `session_id`, `session_key` |
    79|| `session:end` | Session ended (before reset) | `platform`, `user_id`, `session_key` |
    80|| `session:reset` | User ran `/new` or `/reset` | `platform`, `user_id`, `session_key` |
    81|| `agent:start` | Agent begins processing a message | `platform`, `user_id`, `session_id`, `message` |
    82|| `agent:step` | Each iteration of the tool-calling loop | `platform`, `user_id`, `session_id`, `iteration`, `tool_names` |
    83|| `agent:end` | Agent finishes processing | `platform`, `user_id`, `session_id`, `message`, `response` |
    84|| `command:*` | Any slash command executed | `platform`, `user_id`, `command`, `args` |
    85|
    86|#### Wildcard Matching
    87|
    88|Handlers registered for `command:*` fire for any `command:` event (`command:model`, `command:reset`, etc.). Monitor all slash commands with a single subscription.
    89|
    90|### Examples
    91|
    92|#### Telegram Alert on Long Tasks
    93|
    94|Send yourself a message when the agent takes more than 10 steps:
    95|
    96|```yaml
    97|# ~/.hermes/hooks/long-task-alert/HOOK.yaml
    98|name: long-task-alert
    99|description: Alert when agent is taking many steps
   100|events:
   101|  - agent:step
   102|```
   103|
   104|```python
   105|# ~/.hermes/hooks/long-task-alert/handler.py
   106|import os
   107|import httpx
   108|
   109|THRESHOLD = 10
   110|BOT_TOKEN = os.getenv("TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN")
   111|CHAT_ID = os.getenv("TELEGRAM_HOME_CHANNEL")
   112|
   113|async def handle(event_type: str, context: dict):
   114|    iteration = context.get("iteration", 0)
   115|    if iteration == THRESHOLD and BOT_TOKEN and CHAT_ID:
   116|        tools = ", ".join(context.get("tool_names", []))
   117|        text = f"⚠️ Agent has been running for {iteration} steps. Last tools: {tools}"
   118|        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
   119|            await client.post(
   120|                f"https://api.telegram.org/bot{BOT_TOKEN}/sendMessage",
   121|                json={"chat_id": CHAT_ID, "text": text},
   122|            )
   123|```
   124|
   125|#### Command Usage Logger
   126|
   127|Track which slash commands are used:
   128|
   129|```yaml
   130|# ~/.hermes/hooks/command-logger/HOOK.yaml
   131|name: command-logger
   132|description: Log slash command usage
   133|events:
   134|  - command:*
   135|```
   136|
   137|```python
   138|# ~/.hermes/hooks/command-logger/handler.py
   139|import json
   140|from datetime import datetime
   141|from pathlib import Path
   142|
   143|LOG = Path.home() / ".hermes" / "logs" / "command_usage.jsonl"
   144|
   145|def handle(event_type: str, context: dict):
   146|    LOG.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
   147|    entry = {
   148|        "ts": datetime.now().isoformat(),
   149|        "command": context.get("command"),
   150|        "args": context.get("args"),
   151|        "platform": context.get("platform"),
   152|        "user": context.get("user_id"),
   153|    }
   154|    with open(LOG, "a") as f:
   155|        f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")
   156|```
   157|
   158|#### Session Start Webhook
   159|
   160|POST to an external service on new sessions:
   161|
   162|```yaml
   163|# ~/.hermes/hooks/session-webhook/HOOK.yaml
   164|name: session-webhook
   165|description: Notify external service on new sessions
   166|events:
   167|  - session:start
   168|  - session:reset
   169|```
   170|
   171|```python
   172|# ~/.hermes/hooks/session-webhook/handler.py
   173|import httpx
   174|
   175|WEBHOOK_URL = "https://your-service.example.com/hermes-events"
   176|
   177|async def handle(event_type: str, context: dict):
   178|    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
   179|        await client.post(WEBHOOK_URL, json={
   180|            "event": event_type,
   181|            **context,
   182|        }, timeout=5)
   183|```
   184|
   185|### Tutorial: BOOT.md — Run a Startup Checklist on Every Gateway Boot
   186|
   187|A popular pattern from the community: drop a Markdown checklist at `~/.hermes/BOOT.md`, and have the agent run it once every time the gateway starts. Useful for "on every boot, check overnight cron failures and ping me on Discord if anything failed," or "summarize the last 24h of deploy.log and post it to Slack #ops."
   188|
   189|This tutorial shows how to build it yourself as a user-defined hook. Hermes does not ship a built-in BOOT.md hook — you wire up exactly the behavior you want.
   190|
   191|#### What we're building
   192|
   193|1. A file at `~/.hermes/BOOT.md` with natural-language startup instructions.
   194|2. A gateway hook that fires on `gateway:startup`, spawns a one-shot agent with your gateway's resolved model/credentials, and runs the BOOT.md instructions.
   195|3. A `[SILENT]` convention so the agent can opt out of sending a message when there's nothing to report.
   196|
   197|#### Step 1: Write your checklist
   198|
   199|Create `~/.hermes/BOOT.md`. Write it as if you were giving instructions to a human assistant:
   200|
   201|```markdown
   202|# Startup Checklist
   203|
   204|1. Run `hermes cron list` and check if any scheduled jobs failed overnight.
   205|2. If any failed, send a summary to Discord #ops using the `send_message` tool.
   206|3. Check if `/opt/app/deploy.log` has any ERROR lines from the last 24 hours. If yes, summarize them and include in the same Discord message.
   207|4. If nothing went wrong, reply with only `[SILENT]` so no message is sent.
   208|```
   209|
   210|The agent sees this as part of its prompt, so anything you can describe in plain language works — tool calls, shell commands, sending messages, summarizing files.
   211|
   212|#### Step 2: Create the hook
   213|
   214|```text
   215|~/.hermes/hooks/boot-md/
   216|├── HOOK.yaml
   217|└── handler.py
   218|```
   219|
   220|**`~/.hermes/hooks/boot-md/HOOK.yaml`**
   221|
   222|```yaml
   223|name: boot-md
   224|description: Run ~/.hermes/BOOT.md on gateway startup
   225|events:
   226|  - gateway:startup
   227|```
   228|
   229|**`~/.hermes/hooks/boot-md/handler.py`**
   230|
   231|```python
   232|"""Run ~/.hermes/BOOT.md on every gateway startup."""
   233|
   234|import logging
   235|import threading
   236|from pathlib import Path
   237|
   238|logger = logging.getLogger("hooks.boot-md")
   239|
   240|BOOT_FILE = Path.home() / ".hermes" / "BOOT.md"
   241|
   242|
   243|def _build_prompt(content: str) -> str:
   244|    return (
   245|        "You are running a startup boot checklist. Follow the instructions "
   246|        "below exactly.\n\n"
   247|        "---\n"
   248|        f"{content}\n"
   249|        "---\n\n"
   250|        "Execute each instruction. Use the send_message tool to deliver any "
   251|        "messages to platforms like Discord or Slack.\n"
   252|        "If nothing needs attention and there is nothing to report, reply "
   253|        "with ONLY: [SILENT]"
   254|    )
   255|
   256|
   257|def _run_boot_agent(content: str) -> None:
   258|    """Spawn a one-shot agent and execute the checklist.
   259|
   260|    Uses the gateway's resolved model and runtime credentials so this works
   261|    against custom endpoints, aggregators, and OAuth-based providers alike.
   262|    """
   263|    try:
   264|        from gateway.run import _resolve_gateway_model, _resolve_runtime_agent_kwargs
   265|        from run_agent import AIAgent
   266|
   267|        agent = AIAgent(
   268|            model=_resolve_gateway_model(),
   269|            **_resolve_runtime_agent_kwargs(),
   270|            platform="gateway",
   271|            quiet_mode=True,
   272|            skip_context_files=True,
   273|            skip_memory=True,
   274|            max_iterations=20,
   275|        )
   276|        result = agent.run_conversation(_build_prompt(content))
   277|        response = result.get("final_response", "")
   278|        if response and "[SILENT]" not in response:
   279|            logger.info("boot-md completed: %s", response[:200])
   280|        else:
   281|            logger.info("boot-md completed (nothing to report)")
   282|    except Exception as e:
   283|        logger.error("boot-md agent failed: %s", e)
   284|
   285|
   286|async def handle(event_type: str, context: dict) -> None:
   287|    if not BOOT_FILE.exists():
   288|        return
   289|    content = BOOT_FILE.read_text(encoding="utf-8").strip()
   290|    if not content:
   291|        return
   292|
   293|    logger.info("Running BOOT.md (%d chars)", len(content))
   294|
   295|    # Background thread so gateway startup isn't blocked on a full agent turn.
   296|    thread = threading.Thread(
   297|        target=_run_boot_agent,
   298|        args=(content,),
   299|        name="boot-md",
   300|        daemon=True,
   301|    )
   302|    thread.start()
   303|```
   304|
   305|The two key lines:
   306|
   307|- `_resolve_gateway_model()` reads the gateway's currently-configured model.
   308|- `_resolve_runtime_agent_kwargs()` resolves provider credentials the same way a normal gateway turn does — including API keys, base URLs, OAuth tokens, and credential pools.
   309|
   310|Without these, a bare `AIAgent()` falls back to built-in defaults and will 401 against any non-default endpoint.
   311|
   312|#### Step 3: Test it
   313|
   314|Restart the gateway:
   315|
   316|```bash
   317|hermes gateway restart
   318|```
   319|
   320|Watch the logs:
   321|
   322|```bash
   323|hermes logs --follow --level INFO | grep boot-md
   324|```
   325|
   326|You should see `Running BOOT.md (N chars)` followed by either `boot-md completed: ...` (summary of what the agent did) or `boot-md completed (nothing to report)` when the agent replied `[SILENT]`.
   327|
   328|Delete `~/.hermes/BOOT.md` to disable the checklist — the hook stays loaded but silently skips when the file isn't there.
   329|
   330|#### Extending the pattern
   331|
   332|- **Schedule-aware checklists:** key off `datetime.now().weekday()` inside BOOT.md's instructions ("if it's Monday, also check the weekly deploy log"). The instructions are free-form text, so anything the agent can reason about is fair game.
   333|- **Multiple checklists:** point the hook at a different file (`STARTUP.md`, `MORNING.md`, etc.) and register separate hook directories for each.
   334|- **Non-agent variant:** if you don't need a full agent loop, skip `AIAgent` entirely and have the handler post a fixed notification directly via `httpx`. Cheaper, faster, and has no provider dependency.
   335|
   336|#### Why this isn't a built-in
   337|
   338|An earlier version of Hermes shipped this as a built-in hook and silently spawned an agent with bare defaults on every gateway boot. That surprised users with custom endpoints and made the feature invisible to users who didn't know it was running. Keeping it as a documented pattern — built by you, in your hooks directory — means you see exactly what it does and opt in by writing the files.
   339|
   340|### How It Works
   341|
   342|1. On gateway startup, `HookRegistry.discover_and_load()` scans `~/.hermes/hooks/`
   343|2. Each subdirectory with `HOOK.yaml` + `handler.py` is loaded dynamically
   344|3. Handlers are registered for their declared events
   345|4. At each lifecycle point, `hooks.emit()` fires all matching handlers
   346|5. Errors in any handler are caught and logged — a broken hook never crashes the agent
   347|
   348|:::info
   349|Gateway hooks only fire in the **gateway** (Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, Teams). The CLI does not load gateway hooks. For hooks that work everywhere, use [plugin hooks](#plugin-hooks).
   350|:::
   351|
   352|## Plugin Hooks
   353|
   354|[Plugins](/docs/user-guide/features/plugins) can register hooks that fire in **both CLI and gateway** sessions. These are registered programmatically via `ctx.register_hook()` in your plugin's `register()` function.
   355|
   356|```python
   357|def register(ctx):
   358|    ctx.register_hook("pre_tool_call", my_tool_observer)
   359|    ctx.register_hook("post_tool_call", my_tool_logger)
   360|    ctx.register_hook("pre_llm_call", my_memory_callback)
   361|    ctx.register_hook("post_llm_call", my_sync_callback)
   362|    ctx.register_hook("on_session_start", my_init_callback)
   363|    ctx.register_hook("on_session_end", my_cleanup_callback)
   364|```
   365|
   366|**General rules for all hooks:**
   367|
   368|- Callbacks receive **keyword arguments**. Always accept `**kwargs` for forward compatibility — new parameters may be added in future versions without breaking your plugin.
   369|- If a callback **crashes**, it's logged and skipped. Other hooks and the agent continue normally. A misbehaving plugin can never break the agent.
   370|- Two hooks' return values affect behavior: [`pre_tool_call`](#pre_tool_call) can **block** the tool, and [`pre_llm_call`](#pre_llm_call) can **inject context** into the LLM call. All other hooks are fire-and-forget observers.
   371|
   372|### Quick reference
   373|
   374|| Hook | Fires when | Returns |
   375||------|-----------|---------|
   376|| [`pre_tool_call`](#pre_tool_call) | Before any tool executes | `{"action": "block", "message": str}` to veto the call |
   377|| [`post_tool_call`](#post_tool_call) | After any tool returns | ignored |
   378|| [`pre_llm_call`](#pre_llm_call) | Once per turn, before the tool-calling loop | `{"context": str}` to prepend context to the user message |
   379|| [`post_llm_call`](#post_llm_call) | Once per turn, after the tool-calling loop | ignored |
   380|| [`on_session_start`](#on_session_start) | New session created (first turn only) | ignored |
   381|| [`on_session_end`](#on_session_end) | Session ends | ignored |
   382|| [`on_session_finalize`](#on_session_finalize) | CLI/gateway tears down an active session (flush, save, stats) | ignored |
   383|| [`on_session_reset`](#on_session_reset) | Gateway swaps in a fresh session key (e.g. `/new`, `/reset`) | ignored |
   384|| [`subagent_stop`](#subagent_stop) | A `delegate_task` child has exited | ignored |
   385|| [`pre_gateway_dispatch`](#pre_gateway_dispatch) | Gateway received a user message, before auth + dispatch | `{"action": "skip" \| "rewrite" \| "allow", ...}` to influence flow |
   386|| [`pre_approval_request`](#pre_approval_request) | Dangerous command needs user approval, before the prompt/notification is sent | ignored |
   387|| [`post_approval_response`](#post_approval_response) | User responded to an approval prompt (or it timed out) | ignored |
   388|| [`transform_tool_result`](#transform_tool_result) | After any tool returns, before the result is handed back to the model | `str` to replace the result, `None` to leave unchanged |
   389|| [`transform_terminal_output`](#transform_terminal_output) | Inside the `terminal` tool, before truncation/ANSI-strip/redact | `str` to replace the raw output, `None` to leave unchanged |
   390|| [`transform_llm_output`](#transform_llm_output) | After the tool-calling loop completes, before the final response is delivered | `str` to replace the response text, `None`/empty to leave unchanged |
   391|
   392|---
   393|
   394|### `pre_tool_call`
   395|
   396|Fires **immediately before** every tool execution — built-in tools and plugin tools alike.
   397|
   398|**Callback signature:**
   399|
   400|```python
   401|def my_callback(tool_name: str, args: dict, task_id: str, **kwargs):
   402|```
   403|
   404|| Parameter | Type | Description |
   405||-----------|------|-------------|
   406|| `tool_name` | `str` | Name of the tool about to execute (e.g. `"terminal"`, `"web_search"`, `"read_file"`) |
   407|| `args` | `dict` | The arguments the model passed to the tool |
   408|| `task_id` | `str` | Session/task identifier. Empty string if not set. |
   409|
   410|**Fires:** In `model_tools.py`, inside `handle_function_call()`, before the tool's handler runs. Fires once per tool call — if the model calls 3 tools in parallel, this fires 3 times.
   411|
   412|**Return value — veto the call:**
   413|
   414|```python
   415|return {"action": "block", "message": "Reason the tool call was blocked"}
   416|```
   417|
   418|The agent short-circuits the tool with `message` as the error returned to the model. The first matching block directive wins (Python plugins registered first, then shell hooks). Any other return value is ignored, so existing observer-only callbacks keep working unchanged.
   419|
   420|**Use cases:** Logging, audit trails, tool call counters, blocking dangerous operations, rate limiting, per-user policy enforcement.
   421|
   422|**Example — tool call audit log:**
   423|
   424|```python
   425|import json, logging
   426|from datetime import datetime
   427|
   428|logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
   429|
   430|def audit_tool_call(tool_name, args, task_id, **kwargs):
   431|    logger.info("TOOL_CALL session=%s tool=%s args=%s",
   432|                task_id, tool_name, json.dumps(args)[:200])
   433|
   434|def register(ctx):
   435|    ctx.register_hook("pre_tool_call", audit_tool_call)
   436|```
   437|
   438|**Example — warn on dangerous tools:**
   439|
   440|```python
   441|DANGEROUS = {"terminal", "write_file", "patch"}
   442|
   443|def warn_dangerous(tool_name, **kwargs):
   444|    if tool_name in DANGEROUS:
   445|        print(f"⚠ Executing potentially dangerous tool: {tool_name}")
   446|
   447|def register(ctx):
   448|    ctx.register_hook("pre_tool_call", warn_dangerous)
   449|```
   450|
   451|---
   452|
   453|### `post_tool_call`
   454|
   455|Fires **immediately after** every tool execution returns.
   456|
   457|**Callback signature:**
   458|
   459|```python
   460|def my_callback(tool_name: str, args: dict, result: str, task_id: str,
   461|                duration_ms: int, **kwargs):
   462|```
   463|
   464|| Parameter | Type | Description |
   465||-----------|------|-------------|
   466|| `tool_name` | `str` | Name of the tool that just executed |
   467|| `args` | `dict` | The arguments the model passed to the tool |
   468|| `result` | `str` | The tool's return value (always a JSON string) |
   469|| `task_id` | `str` | Session/task identifier. Empty string if not set. |
   470|| `duration_ms` | `int` | How long the tool's dispatch took, in milliseconds (measured with `time.monotonic()` around `registry.dispatch()`). |
   471|
   472|**Fires:** In `model_tools.py`, inside `handle_function_call()`, after the tool's handler returns. Fires once per tool call. Does **not** fire if the tool raised an unhandled exception (the error is caught and returned as an error JSON string instead, and `post_tool_call` fires with that error string as `result`).
   473|
   474|**Return value:** Ignored.
   475|
   476|**Use cases:** Logging tool results, metrics collection, tracking tool success/failure rates, latency dashboards, per-tool budget alerts, sending notifications when specific tools complete.
   477|

   481|from collections import Counter, defaultdict
   482|import json
   483|
   484|_tool_counts = Counter()
   485|_error_counts = Counter()
   486|_latency_ms = defaultdict(list)
   487|
   488|def track_metrics(tool_name, result, duration_ms=0, **kwargs):
   489|    _tool_counts[tool_name] += 1
   490|    _latency_ms[tool_name].append(duration_ms)
   491|    try:
   492|        parsed = json.loads(result)
   493|        if "error" in parsed:
   494|            _error_counts[tool_name] += 1
   495|    except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError):
   496|        pass
   497|
   498|def register(ctx):
   499|    ctx.register_hook("post_tool_call", track_metrics)
   500|```

{"path": "/home/marco/.hermes/hooks/boot-md/handler.py", "content": """"Run ~/.hermes/BOOT.md on every gateway startup."""

import logging
import threading
from pathlib import Path

logger = logging.getLogger("hooks.boot-md")

BOOT_FILE = Path.home() / ".hermes" / "BOOT.md"


def _build_prompt(content: str) -> str:
    return (
        "You are running a startup boot checklist. Follow the instructions "
        "below exactly.\n\n"
        "---\n"
        f"{content}\n"
        "---\n\n"
        "Execute each instruction. Use the send_message tool to deliver any "
        "messages to platforms like Discord or Telegram.\n"
        "If nothing needs attention and there is nothing to report, reply "
        "with ONLY: [SILENT]"
    )


def _run_boot_agent(content: str) -> None:
    """Spawn a one-shot agent and execute the checklist.

    Uses the gateway's resolved model and runtime credentials so this works
    against custom endpoints, aggregators, and OAuth-based providers alike.
    """
    try:
        from gateway.run import _resolve_gateway_model, _resolve_runtime_agent_kwargs
        from run_agent import AIAgent

        agent = AIAgent(
            model=_resolve_gateway_model(),
            **_resolve_runtime_agent_kwargs(),
            platform="gateway",
            quiet_mode=True,
            skip_context_files=True,
            skip_memory=True,
            max_iterations=20,
        )
        result = agent.run_conversation(_build_prompt(content))
        response = result.get("final_response", "")
        if response and "[SILENT]" not in response:
            logger.info("boot-md completed: %s", response[:200])
        else:
            logger.info("boot-md completed (nothing to report)")
    except Exception as e:
        logger.error("boot-md agent failed: %s", e)


async def handle(event_type: str, context: dict) -> None:
    if not BOOT_FILE.exists():
        return
    content = BOOT_FILE.read_text(encoding="utf-8").strip()
    if not content:
        return

    logger.info("Running BOOT.md (%d chars)", len(content))

    # Background thread so gateway startup isn't blocked on a full agent turn.
    thread = threading.Thread(
        target=_run_boot_agent,
        args=(content,),
        name="boot-md",
        daemon=True,
    )
    thread.start()
"}
